title: Program, Portfolio & Benefits Realization
summary: The levels above the single project and how outputs become measurable benefits and strategic value. Supports the PMBOK 8 Adopt a Holistic View and Focus on Value principles: see the whole system of work, and treat value - not just delivery - as the measure of success.

sections.heading: Systems thinking and value
sections.body: Programs and portfolios are where the Adopt a Holistic View and Focus on Value principles do their work. Adopt a Holistic View says to recognise and respond to interactions across the whole system rather than optimising one project in isolation. Focus on Value says value is the ultimate measure of success, so the point of the work is the benefit it realises, not the deliverable it ships.

blocks.title: How the principles map
lines: Adopt a Holistic View -> coordinate interdependencies across projects, programs and operations.
Focus on Value -> success is measured by realised benefit, not output shipped.
Programs manage benefits and interdependencies; portfolios choose the right work for strategy.

sections.heading: Project vs program vs portfolio
sections.body: A project delivers a specific output within a defined effort. A program is a group of related projects, subsidiary programs and program activities managed together to obtain benefits not available from managing them separately. A portfolio is the collection of projects, programs and operations selected and prioritised to meet the organisation's strategic objectives.

rows.value:
Focus: a defined output or deliverable.
Goal: complete the effort and hand over the result.

Focus: interdependencies between related components.
Goal: coordinate them for shared benefit not available separately.

Focus: strategic alignment and prioritisation.
Goal: select and fund the right work to meet strategy.

blocks.title: Exam cue
lines: Program = coordination for benefit.
Portfolio = doing the right work to meet strategy.
Wording about interdependencies -> program; wording about choosing/funding the right work -> portfolio.

sections.heading: Outputs vs outcomes vs benefits
sections.body: These three terms form a value chain and are easy to confuse on the exam. A project can deliver every planned output and still realise no benefit if the outcome does not occur or is never measured.

items.text: The thing produced - a new system, product or capability.
The change the output enables - faster processing or a new behaviour.
The measurable value the organisation gains from that change - reduced cost or increased revenue.

blocks.title: Exam cue
lines: Output enables an outcome; the outcome produces the benefit.

sections.heading: Benefits realization management
sections.body: Benefits are managed across a lifecycle that usually outlasts the project itself. Because benefits often accrue after the project closes, an owner in operations must continue to track them.

items.title: Identify
lines: Name the expected benefits.
Link them to strategic goals.

items.title: Plan
lines: Capture in a benefits management plan.
Name an owner, metrics, baseline and target, and a timeline.

items.title: Execute / deliver
lines: Deliver the outputs that enable the benefits.

items.title: Transition
lines: Hand the result into operations so the change can take hold.

items.title: Sustain / measure
lines: Track benefits over time against the agreed metrics.

blocks.title: Exam cue
lines: If no owner tracks benefits after closure, they are likely never realised.

sections.heading: Tangible vs intangible benefits
sections.body: Both are real. Good benefits planning defines metrics for both so progress can be shown - including proxy metrics for the ones that are harder to measure.

groups.label: Nature
lines: Financial or directly measurable.
Easy to quantify and put in a business case.

groups.label: Examples
lines: Cost savings.
Additional revenue.
Faster cycle time.
Reduced defects.

groups.label: Nature
lines: Real but harder to measure.
Still needs agreed proxy metrics.

groups.label: Examples
lines: Improved reputation and brand.
Higher staff morale.
Better customer goodwill.
New organisational capability.

blocks.title: Exam cue
lines: Intangible does not mean unmeasurable; agree proxy metrics up front.

sections.heading: Value over the iron triangle
sections.body: Benefits realization is one of the strongest drivers of genuine project success and reflects the value focus of PMBOK Guide 7 and 8. The purpose of any project is to create value for the organisation and its stakeholders, not simply to produce a deliverable. A project delivered on time, in scope and on budget can still fail if it realises no benefit.

blocks.title: Exam cue
lines: On time, on budget and in scope can still be a failure if no value is delivered.
Do not confuse iron-triangle success with value; meeting constraints is necessary but not sufficient.

source: PM Cram study notes - reworded and restructured for the current ECO-based PMP exam, aligned to PMBOK 8 principles.
